title: The Size of the Problem
bullets: In 1914 the Russian Empire covered about 8.6 million square miles, a sixth of all land on the planet, more than twice the size of the entire United States including Alaska.
Three load-bearing parts ran the machine: autocracy, the doctrine that the tsar's will was the only legitimate authority and any limit on it a kind of blasphemy.
Orthodoxy made Moscow the self-styled Third Rome after Constantinople fell to the Ottomans in 1453, with the church an arm of the state rather than a check on the tsar.
By the 1649 Ulozhenie a serf could be bought, sold, mortgaged, gambled away, and flogged, owing the lord barshchina labor or obrok payments.
By the early nineteenth century around 23 million people, more than a third of the population, were privately owned serfs, several times the four million enslaved in the American South.
The book's thesis: the empire was a machine for converting the unfreedom of the many into the grandeur of the few, and the wager could not hold forever.
The state Peter inherits is not yet an empire but Muscovy, the Grand Duchy of Moscow, grown out of Kievan Rus' and freed from the Tatar yoke of the Golden Horde.
The fuse on the wager was lit on a December morning in 1825 and burned for ninety-two years to February 1917.

title: The Boy Who Saw the Spears
bullets: Pyotr Alekseyevich Romanov was born on 9 June 1672, the first child of Tsar Alexei's second wife Natalya Naryshkina, and became Peter the Great.
After Fyodor III died childless in 1682 the Miloslavskys and Naryshkins fought over the disabled Ivan and the nine-year-old Peter.
In May 1682 the streltsy stormed the Kremlin and butchered leading Naryshkins, including Artamon Matveyev, in front of the nine-year-old Peter, the central psychological fact of the book.
Both boys were crowned as Ivan V and Peter I joint tsars, with Sophia Alekseyevna ruling as regent from 1682, the first woman to hold Russian power in her own right.
Sophia's minister Vasily Golitsyn signed the 1689 Treaty of Nerchinsk with Qing China but lost two campaigns against the Crimean Tatars.
Exiled to Preobrazhenskoye, Peter raised the Preobrazhensky and Semyonovsky play regiments and learned shipbuilding from foreigners in the German Quarter.
Peter outlasted Sophia in 1689 after fleeing to the Trinity Monastery, ruled alone from Ivan's death in 1696, and won Azov in 1696 only after building a fleet over one winter.
In 1697 the tsar of Russia left the country in disguise to learn a trade, the only ruler in the book to do so.

title: The Window
bullets: The Grand Embassy of about 250 people set out in 1697; Peter traveled incognito as carpenter Peter Mikhailov and worked the Dutch East India Company shipyards in Amsterdam.
He toured the Royal Society, the Mint where Isaac Newton was Warden, and Parliament, an institution England had since 1265 and Russia would not get for two centuries.
On his return in 1698 Peter executed more than a thousand streltsy, left the bodies on display for months, and personally cut off his boyars' beards.
The Great Northern War against Sweden's Charles XII ran twenty-one years and began with the 1700 catastrophe at the Battle of Narva.
In 1703 Peter founded St Petersburg on a frozen swamp, his window to Europe, built by conscripted labor at a death toll proverbially called the city built on bones.
At the Battle of Poltava on 8 July 1709 Peter destroyed Charles XII's army; Sweden's century as a great power ended and Russia's began.
Peter overreached in the 1711 Pruth River campaign, escaping disaster only by handing Azov back to the Ottomans.
The new navy won at Gangut in 1714, and the 1721 Treaty of Nystad gave Russia the eastern Baltic coast and made it a European great power.

title: Revolution From Above
bullets: Peter built a rationalized bureaucratic state copied from Sweden: the Governing Senate in 1711, the Colleges, provinces, and a parallel surveillance system of fiskaly and the Procurator-General.
The Table of Ranks of 1722 made status flow from service to the state, converting the hereditary boyar class into a service class owing its privileges to the crown.
The soul tax of 1718-1724 levied a flat tax on every individual male soul, deepening serfdom by fixing every peasant in place as a tax-unit.
The 1705 beard tax and Western-dress orders landed as an assault on the soul of Orthodox Russia, who called Peter the Antichrist.
Peter left the Patriarchate vacant from 1700 and abolished it in 1721, replacing it with the Holy Synod run by a lay Ober-Procurator until 1917.
The Old Believers, who had split over Patriarch Nikon's 1650s reforms, were doubly persecuted, taxed punitively, and driven to the empire's edges where they became successful merchants.
Peter reformed the calendar in 1700, simplified the alphabet into a civil script, started the newspaper Vedomosti, and chartered the Russian Academy of Sciences, which opened in 1725.
Peter died in February 1725 at fifty-two, having had his son Alexei tortured to death in 1718 and leaving no heir, opening thirty-seven years of Guards-made rulers.

title: The Age of the Guards
bullets: For thirty-seven years Russian successions were settled by which faction the Guards backed, the Preobrazhensky and Semyonovsky regiments Peter had raised as a boy.
Catherine I, Peter's servant-born second wife, ruled 1725-1727 with Menshikov effectively running the country before his spectacular fall and exile to Siberia.
On Anna's accession in 1730 the Supreme Privy Council offered the throne only if she signed written Conditions limiting her power; she signed, then tore them up.
The infant Ivan VI, proclaimed emperor in 1740, was deposed at fifteen months and kept in secret solitary confinement until killed by his guards in 1764.
Elizabeth seized the throne in 1741 by personally leading the Preobrazhensky grenadiers from their barracks, and founded Moscow University in 1755.
Mikhail Lomonosov, an Arctic fisherman's son, became a chemist, physicist, astronomer, and poet, proof the top-down knowledge machine had begun making Russians not just importing Germans.
Bartolomeo Rastrelli gave Russian grandeur its definitive look with the Winter Palace and the Catherine Palace, sublime surfaces raised by a serf economy.
Russia beat Frederick the Great repeatedly in the Seven Years' War, but Peter III's 1762 peace, the Miracle of the House of Brandenburg, saved Prussia.
Peter III's 1762 Manifesto on the Freedom of the Nobility abolished compulsory noble service while leaving the serf bound, the precise structural injustice of the next 155 years.

title: The German Girl Who Became Russia
bullets: Born Sophie of Anhalt-Zerbst in 1729 with no Russian blood, she became Catherine the Great, the most formidable ruler Russia had between Peter and the twentieth century.
Catherine's coup of 1762, organized by the Orlov brothers and the Guards, overthrew her husband Peter III, who died days later in custody.
She corresponded for decades with Voltaire, bought Diderot's library and paid him to keep it, and founded the Hermitage in the Winter Palace.
She presented herself to Europe as the showcase of enlightened absolutism, though patronizing Voltaire was cheaper than freeing a serf and bought a better European press.
Catherine personally wrote the Nakaz, lifted almost word for word from Montesquieu and Beccaria, while Joseph II abolished judicial torture and she changed nothing.
The 1767 Legislative Commission of 564 deputies, pointedly excluding serfs, debated for eighteen months and produced no new law code.
Catherine intellectually conceded the case against serfdom and concluded she could not touch it, choosing Frederick's path over Joseph II's emancipation of 1781.
She had herself publicly inoculated against smallpox in 1768, a genuinely bold scientific gesture, and gave the Academy directorship to Princess Yekaterina Dashkova.

title: Pugachev and the Machinery of Bondage
bullets: A privately owned serf around 1770 was advertised for sale in newspapers, owed barshchina or obrok, and by a 1767 decree could be sentenced to hard labor for petitioning against his master.
Roughly 23 million private serfs, a bound population larger than all of Britain, were held not on distant plantations but in the villages that fed the capital.
Russia abolished serfdom in 1861, before the United States freed its slaves by a war that killed 750,000 and after Britain's 1833 abolition, the least industrial acting earliest without war.
Yemelyan Pugachev, a Don Cossack deserter, declared in 1773 that he was the murdered Peter III returned to free the people, weaponizing the returned-true-tsar template.
Pugachev's Rebellion of 1773-1775 was the largest popular uprising before the twentieth century, raising armies tens of thousands strong and burning Kazan.
Pugachev was betrayed, caged, taken to Moscow, and publicly executed in January 1775; even the Yaik river was renamed the Ural to erase him.
The revolt moved Catherine the other way: in 1785 the Charter to the Nobility codified absolute property in serfs while leaving the serf with fewer rights than ever.
In 1790 Alexander Radishchev published A Journey from St Petersburg to Moscow; Catherine called him worse than Pugachev and exiled him, and jailed the publisher Nikolai Novikov.

title: The Empire Doubles
bullets: By 1796 Catherine had added some 200,000 square miles and seven million people and wiped a thousand-year-old European country off the map.
A Russian fleet sailed the length of Europe into the Mediterranean and burned an Ottoman fleet at Chesma in 1770, the first proof Russian sea power could reach there.
The 1774 Treaty of Kuchuk-Kainarji gave Russia a Black Sea navy and a claim to protect Orthodox Christians in the Ottoman Empire, the lever that lit the Crimean War.
Catherine annexed Crimea outright in 1783, the historic completion of Russia's contest with the steppe slave-raiders and a triumph that Istanbul read as the start of decline.
Grigory Potemkin governed Novorossiya, founded cities including Odessa, and gave history the durable myth of the Potemkin villages, largely hostile propaganda.
Alexander Suvorov, who never lost a battle, stormed the Ottoman fortress of Izmail in 1790 in the second Russo-Turkish War.
In three Partitions of Poland in 1772, 1793, and 1795, Russia, Prussia, and Austria erased the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth for 123 years despite Kosciuszko's 1794 uprising.
Russian fur traders established the first permanent settlements in Russian America in the 1780s and 1790s, reaching down the Pacific coast to northern California.

title: The Mad Tsar and the Hopeful One
bullets: Paul I, embittered by his mother Catherine sidelining him for thirty-four years, ruled 1796-1801 like a man settling scores.
Paul's 1797 Pauline Law of Succession abolished Peter's disastrous 1722 rule and fixed strict male primogeniture, ending disputed successions and Guards-made emperors.
Paul lurched in foreign policy, sending Suvorov across the Alps in 1799 then ordering a Cossack army toward British India, and was strangled in his bedroom in March 1801.
Alexander I, who had given conditional blessing to his father's removal, began his reign as a guilt-shadowed probable accomplice to the murder.
Alexander I, soaked in Enlightenment ideals by his tutor Frederic-Cesar de La Harpe, gathered the Unofficial Committee and privately discussed a constitution and ending serfdom.
Mikhail Speransky, a priest's son who rose by genius, drafted a comprehensive plan for a law-governed constitutional monarchy that was almost entirely shelved.
Beaten at Austerlitz in 1805 and Friedland in 1807, Alexander signed the humiliating Treaty of Tilsit on a raft in the Niemen and joined the Continental System.
Alexander expanded anyway, taking Finland as an autonomous Grand Duchy in 1809 and Bessarabia in 1812 before Napoleon's army massed on the western border.
In June 1812 Napoleon Bonaparte crossed the Niemen with the Grande Armee of around 600,000 men, the largest army Europe had ever assembled.
Barclay de Tolly refused the decisive battle and retreated deep into the country, burning supplies, the same strategy that had beaten Charles XII at Poltava.
At the Battle of Borodino on 7 September 1812, one of the bloodiest single days before the twentieth century, around 70,000 to 75,000 fell and Kutuzov withdrew intact.
At the council of Fili, Kutuzov chose to abandon Moscow, judging that losing Moscow was not losing Russia but losing the army would be.
The burning of Moscow destroyed perhaps two thirds of the city; a deliberate Russian element, probably ordered by governor Rostopchin, is widely accepted.
The Grande Armee was gutted by distance, hunger, and typhus before the frosts; the crossing of the Berezina in late November left tens of thousands dead.
The campaign of 1813-1814 turned the war on Napoleon; the Battle of Leipzig in October 1813 broke his hold on Germany and the allies entered Paris in 1814.
Russia reached the summit of its grandeur having changed nothing internally: the serf who marched to Paris and saw a Europe without serfdom came home still owned.

title: Gendarme of Europe
bullets: At the Congress of Vienna Alexander I was the dominant figure; Russia gained Congress Poland, to which he granted a written constitution and an elected Sejm.
Alexander proposed the Holy Alliance, which became the ideological flag of the post-1815 order and made Russia the gendarme of Europe, the continent's conservative cop.
Count Arakcheyev's military colonies turned whole districts of state peasants into permanent military settlements under total regimentation, producing brutal revolts.
In 1820 a Russian naval expedition under Bellingshausen made what many rank as the first confirmed sighting of Antarctica.
Alexander I died suddenly in November 1825 at Taganrog, spawning the legend that he had become the Siberian holy man Fyodor Kuzmich.
A confused succession left it unclear for three weeks who the emperor was, opening a gap the Decembrists seized.
The Decembrists were aristocratic army officers who had marched to Paris and read the European liberalism their emperor suppressed, the Russian beat of a continent-wide drum.
The Decembrist revolt failed in a single afternoon on 14 December 1825 when Nicholas I fired grapeshot into Senate Square; five ringleaders were hanged.
The revolt changed nothing in 1825 and everything afterward, the moment the fuse was lit and a torch passed to every later revolutionary in the book.

title: Official Nationality
bullets: Nicholas I became tsar ordering grapeshot fired at the Decembrists and spent thirty years making sure 14 December 1825 never happened again.
Sergei Uvarov formulated the doctrine of Official Nationality in 1833 as the triad Orthodoxy, Autocracy, Nationality, the regime's own statement of its thesis.
Nicholas created the Third Section, the empire's first true centralized political police, direct ancestor of the Okhrana and the Soviet security organs.
When the Poles rose in the November Uprising of 1830-1831 Nicholas crushed it, abolished the Polish constitution, and ruled Poland as occupied territory.
The most repressive reign was the foundational age of Russian literature because the novel became the only place a thinking Russian could examine the country.
Alexander Pushkin, who created the modern Russian literary language and wrote Eugene Onegin, died in 1837 from a duel; Lermontov was killed in a duel in 1841.
Nikolai Gogol's Dead Souls satirized a society that literally counts and trades human beings, getting past the censor partly because it was funny.
The intelligentsia split into Westernizers and Slavophiles, the master template of Russian intellectual life ever after.
In 1849 the Third Section arrested the Petrashevsky Circle; twenty-one including the young Fyodor Dostoevsky were marched to a mock execution before Siberian hard labor.

title: The War That Told the Truth
bullets: The Crimean War of 1853-1856 was the experiment that tested the regime's claim that autocracy and unfreedom produced strength, and the claim failed in public on Russian soil.
Nicholas I inflated a quarrel over the holy sites in Jerusalem into a confrontation, misjudging that Britain, France, and Austria would not let him dismember the Ottomans.
The two great sick empires of the nineteenth century fought the same war on opposite sides and both learned their survival depended on Western industrial powers.
The siege of Sevastopol lasted nearly a year; the surgeon Nikolai Pirogov pioneered battlefield triage and ether anesthesia and the artillery officer Leo Tolstoy wrote the Sevastopol Sketches.
Russia had almost no railways and moved troops to Crimea by ox-cart over dirt roads, specifically behind the industrial-policy states it had watched modernize for two decades.
Nicholas I died in February 1855 a broken man, a persistent rumor he took poison rejected by most historians as too shapely.
The 1856 Treaty of Paris barred Russia from keeping a navy on the Black Sea and publicly demoted the gendarme of Europe.
Roughly a quarter to a third of a million Russians died, mostly of disease, and the war killed the regime's central belief about itself.

title: The Tsar Liberator
bullets: Alexander II told the reluctant Moscow nobility in 1856 it was better to abolish serfdom from above than wait for it to abolish itself from below.
The Emancipation of the Serfs of 19 February 1861 freed around 23 million privately owned serfs, the single largest act of legislative liberation in history to that point.
Russia freed a bound population several times the American one, peacefully, two years before Lincoln's Proclamation and four years before the Thirteenth Amendment.
Russian emancipation came before industrialization as a political pre-emption of revolt, while British abolition came after industry no longer needed slaves.
Freed peasants received less land than they had farmed, owed forty-nine years of redemption payments, and were bound to the village commune, the mir.
Peasants who expected the land free reacted with anger; at Bezdna scores were shot for not believing the emancipation they were handed was the real one.
The Great Reforms followed: the 1864 zemstvo reform created elected local councils and the 1864 judicial reform built independent courts and trial by jury.
Milyutin's military reforms of 1874 replaced 25-year serf conscription with universal short service, but Alexander II refused every request for a national parliament.

title: Going to the People
bullets: The disappointment of the Emancipation convinced a generation that the autocracy could not be reformed and must be destroyed.
Turgenev's 1862 Fathers and Sons gave the world the word nihilist; Chernyshevsky's What Is to Be Done?, written in a prison cell, became the radicals' bible.
In 1874 thousands of students went to the people in the countryside; the devout, monarchist peasants often tied up the agitators and marched them to the police.
Land and Liberty split in 1879 into Black Repartition under Plekhanov and the larger People's Will, which adopted systematic political assassination as a theorized program.
Karakozov fired the first shot at Alexander II in 1866; in 1878 a St Petersburg jury astonishingly acquitted Vera Zasulich after she shot the city's governor.
The People's Will smuggled dynamite into the Winter Palace itself in the 1880 bombing, killing about a dozen soldiers while the tsar's late dinner saved him.
Alexander II approved the modest Loris-Melikov project on the morning of 1 March 1881, the smallest crack toward national representation.
That afternoon a second bomber killed him; the assassination of Alexander II produced not revolution but the harshest reaction since Nicholas I.

title: The Golden Age
bullets: The same empire being hunted in the surrounding chapters produced the greatest concentration of literature any country has produced in a comparable span.
Leo Tolstoy wrote War and Peace and Anna Karenina, then renounced rank, property, and the church, influencing a young lawyer named Gandhi by correspondence.
Fyodor Dostoevsky came back from a Siberian camp to write Crime and Punishment, Demons, and The Brothers Karamazov with its Grand Inquisitor parable.
Anton Chekhov, a doctor and the grandson of a serf who bought his own freedom, reinvented the short story and wrote The Cherry Orchard for the Moscow Art Theatre.
The Mighty Handful built a Russian music from folk song against the conservatory-trained Tchaikovsky, whose 1812 Overture gave the West its image of Russia beating Napoleon.
The Wanderers walked out of the Imperial Academy of Arts in 1863 to paint the real Russia; the merchant Pavel Tretyakov assembled and gave Moscow the national collection.
Dmitri Mendeleev published the periodic table in 1869, vindicated when gallium and germanium matched his predicted gaps, and Lobachevsky created non-Euclidean geometry.
Ivan Pavlov won a Nobel Prize in 1904 and founded a science of conditioned reflexes, all produced inside the same empire that hanged the men of the previous chapter.

title: The Empire of All the Russias
bullets: By the 1897 census only about 44 percent of roughly 125 million people counted as Great Russians; the empire was a vast multi-ethnic patchwork.
The Caucasian War, dated from around 1817 to 1864, produced Imam Shamil, who held the Muslim mountains for a quarter century before his capture in 1859.
In the 1860s the Russian state drove the bulk of the Circassians off their land into the sea, with deaths in the hundreds of thousands, described by many as genocide.
Russian armies took Tashkent in 1865, stormed the Khanate of Khiva in 1873, and annexed Merv in 1884, nearly fighting Britain at the 1885 Panjdeh incident.
Russia sold Alaska to the United States in 1867 for 7.2 million dollars, derided as Seward's Folly, because land at the edge was negotiable when central power never was.
The Trans-Siberian Railway, begun in 1891 and running about 5,700 miles, was the physical answer to the impossible size that opened the book.
Russification pressed Poles, Ukrainians, and Finns to adopt Russian; the 1876 Ems Ukaz banned Ukrainian books and hardened the very nationalisms that helped tear the empire apart.
Russia confined most of its Jews to the Pale of Settlement and after 1881 a wave of pogroms drove the largest Jewish emigration in history and pushed many into revolution.

title: The Counter-Reformation
bullets: Alexander III ruled 1881-1894 spending thirteen years destroying his murdered father's reforms on the theory that liberating anything had been the mistake.
Konstantin Pobedonostsev, the regime's chief ideologue, called democracy the great lie of our time and drafted the manifesto burying the Loris-Melikov near-constitution.
The Counter-Reforms narrowed the zemstvo franchise, created the land captain over peasants, crushed university autonomy, and built the highly effective Okhrana.
Sergei Witte's state-driven industrialization put Russia on the gold standard in 1897 and used List's protective-tariff infant-industry playbook financed by French and Belgian capital.
The Baku oil fields briefly made the empire the world's largest oil producer; industrial output grew at among the fastest rates in the world.
Witte built an urban proletariat with no unions or political voice while Pobedonostsev guaranteed it had no legal way to express a single demand.
The Great Famine of 1891-1892 killed hundreds of thousands amid the global Long Depression and drove the intelligentsia decisively into opposition.
Marxism arrived via Plekhanov's Emancipation of Labour group; the RSDLP's 1903 split created Lenin's Bolsheviks and the Mensheviks, and the SRs revived assassination.

title: The Year It Cracked
bullets: Nicholas II, the last tsar, ruled 1894-1917 believing God had entrusted him with unlimited power he must hand undiminished to his son.
At his 1896 coronation a crowd crush at Khodynka field killed over a thousand people while the tsar attended a French embassy ball that evening.
The Russo-Japanese War of 1904-1905 delivered the Crimean lesson again: Japan took Port Arthur, won at Mukden, and annihilated the Baltic Fleet at Tsushima.
On 9 January 1905 troops fired on Father Gapon's peaceful petition march to the Winter Palace; Bloody Sunday killed the sacred myth of the tsar-father.
The Revolution of 1905 brought strikes, the Potemkin mutiny, and the St Petersburg Soviet led by Trotsky, forcing the October Manifesto and a State Duma.
The 1906 Fundamental Laws reasserted autocratic power, the first two Dumas were dissolved, and the 1907 election law was illegally rewritten in a coup.
Pyotr Stolypin paired ferocious repression with land reforms to break the commune, needing twenty years; he was shot at the Kiev opera in 1911 by an Okhrana informer.
The dynasty's survival came to rest on the secret hemophilia of the Tsarevich Alexei and Rasputin's influence over the frightened empress.
In 1913 the regime staged the magnificent Romanov Tercentenary, the grandeur performing confidence over a state with a hollow center.

title: The Fuse Reaches the Powder
bullets: A regime that had survived two and a third centuries took six weeks in 1917 to disintegrate; the First World War applied an unbearable load to a structure with a fatal flaw.
Russia entered the First World War in August 1914 and renamed its capital Petrograd; soldiers were sent to the front without enough rifles.
The 1914 Battle of Tannenberg destroyed an entire Russian army; General Samsonov shot himself rather than report the scale of the disaster.
The 1916 Brusilov offensive showed the army could still fight superbly when led, bleeding it white for gains the system could not exploit.
Nicholas II took personal command of the army in 1915, welding his sacred person to every defeat and leaving the capital to the empress and Rasputin.
Aristocrats fronted by Prince Felix Yusupov murdered Rasputin in December 1916, concluding the dynasty was destroying itself.
The February Revolution began as a bread queue and strike; it ended when the Petrograd garrison refused to fire and joined the crowd.
Dual power split between the Provisional Government and the reborn Petrograd Soviet, whose Order No. 1 showed where the guns answered.
On 2 March 1917 in a railway carriage at Pskov, Nicholas II signed his abdication for himself and his son, ending the Romanov dynasty after three hundred years.
The Provisional Government lost the streets to the Bolsheviks Lenin had built from the 1903 split, who took power in October and dispersed the Constituent Assembly.
